reviews.author: EVGENY IVANOV
reviews.text: I really enjoyed visiting this place. It was quite an interesting and atmospheric experience to see the giant white Buddha and the beautiful architecture of the temple. The views from the top are definitely worth the climb, and it feels very peaceful despite the tourists. The only downsides were the cleanliness in certain areas—it could definitely be maintained a bit better. Also, while the toilet is free, it was in a terrible, unhygienic condition. However, overall, the cultural and spiritual value of the site makes it a must-visit in Nha Trang. A solid 5 stars for the experience itself!

reviews.author: Mark
reviews.text: Long Son Pagoda is undoubtedly a beautiful pagoda, much like many others throughout Vietnam. The the setting is meaningful, and it carries spiritual value for both locals and visitors. The pagoda itself is not the issue. What I personally find disappointing is the policy and organization around it. The overall setup feels overly commercial. Incense sticks, for example, should be freely available as they are in many other pagodas. Donations should come from the heart of visitors who come seeking peace, support, or blessings, not from a structure where everything must be purchased. A pagoda is not a marketplace; it is a spiritual sanctuary. It is also not appropriate that scooters and motorbikes are allowed to drive between visitors on the grounds. This disturbs the tranquility and undermines the spiritual atmosphere. Vehicles should be parked outside the pagoda area so that people can walk around calmly and experience the space in peace. My criticism is not directed at the pagoda itself, but at the way it is managed. It is simply unfortunate to see spiritual places gradually turning into commercial environments, when their true strength lies in serenity, simplicity, and sincere devotion. I genuinely hope that, in the future, the spiritual essence will once again be placed at the center of the experience.

reviews.author: G Whizz
reviews.text: Long Son Pagoda is one of the most impressive sights in Nha Trang and, in my view, an absolute must-see. The complex is well maintained and visually striking, with a peaceful atmosphere despite being close to the city. Climbing up to the large white Buddha is well worth the effort, as the views over Nha Trang are excellent and the setting feels calm and spiritual. The mix of architecture, statues, and greenery makes the visit both culturally interesting and relaxing. A highlight of the city and highly recommended.

reviews.author: Colin Humphreys
reviews.text: This is a free to enter site. IF you want to go inside the temple, short sleeved shirts and shorts are not allowed. If you intend to go inside, just remember to take/ wear suitable attire. There are about 220 steps, in total on the way up to the top, and there's a reclining Buddha on the way up that will break up the climb a little. There is also a road to the right hand side on the way up, so you could walk up that way. You should choose the steps for one way, I think, so that you get in the reclining Buddha and the bell tower as well.

reviews.author: Mike Smith
reviews.text: Gorgeous buddha statue to visit. Free entry. No need to cover knees if visiting buddha but cover your knees and shoulders if you intend visiting the temple on site. Quite a few steps to climb to get to the top but plenty of stop and catch your breath places. Vendors selling cold drinks on site. Good view of nha trang beach from up there.

reviews.author: Zsolt Szoke Torok
reviews.text: At the bottom of the hill there is a temple, which can only be entered wearing knee-length clothing and barefoot. You have to climb 200 steps to reach the huge white Buddha. Beautiful panorama of Nha Trang. The building itself is huge. You can enter from the back, where there is an altar. There is no entrance fee.

reviews.author: Denise Hernández- Wright
reviews.text: Lovely peaceful place to visit. Not much going on in 40 mins you are done but it's certainly very pretty up there and views are great. Closes at 5 pm but when went after and got it. Free entry. Please be respectful.

reviews.author: Fran Burke
reviews.text: The temple complex is beautiful, with a huge seated Buddha at the top of the mountain and a big reclining Buddha in a garden below the summit. There's an excellent, cheap Chay (vegetarian) restaurant in the temple complex, open until 8 pm.

reviews.author: Jacque Lane
reviews.text: It’s a don’t miss on your trip to nah Trang. It’s free to enter. The temple is beautiful. The Buddha is stunning as well as the reclining Buddha. A joy to see the monks in the monastery. The sad thing is the trash all around and the once lovely railings in disrepair. It’s surprising that there isn’t an effort to clean up the grounds. I hope the donations are enough to do so.

reviews.author: Asaf N
reviews.text: Beautiful historic site with plenty of photo spots and ancient details to explore. Just keep in mind there is an entrance fee of 100kđ, but it’s still worth the visit if you enjoy culture and architecture a bit

reviews.author: Tallow Marble
reviews.text: Recommend a visit, remember to wear clothes covering shoulders and proper footwear since it's a Buddhist temple, respectful. If visiting Nha Trang, recommend you give it a visit

reviews.author: Gemma Clough
reviews.text: Really lovely place to visit Totally free but make sure you’re covered up. Lots of steps and you can see the lying down Buddha and the tall statue.

reviews.author: Baktybala Torgautova
reviews.text: Beautiful temple and Buddha on the top of the mountain. Meet to climb up a bit. Beautiful view from the top. On the way you can buy different souvenirs, cool drinks. I went around lunch time, not that busy. On the way back from the village.

reviews.author: Jessica T Phan
reviews.text: It’s beautiful 100 years old biggest Buddah statues in Vietnam, very well maintained over 100 years since. If you would like to donate, make few steps to right side where the big looked door with the slot open. The guide sign is faded, its barely noticeable how to make donations. The pagoda itself quite touristic and not much to see except these statues

reviews.author: Y. T.
reviews.text: Be ready to climb lots of steep stairs, have physical strength to climb to the top and be out of breath! No heels are recommended. White Buddha is gorgeous and rewarding to look at. Some ladies sell incense sticks but they don’t sell matches/lighters; you must borrow one from another tourist if you don’t have a lighter. I think there is a path for cars to go near the top if you’re not into climbing stairs.

reviews.author: Taisiia Meshcher
reviews.text: It’s definitely a must-visit. The largest Buddha statue in Vietnam (24m). Crowded but still possible to take good pics.

reviews.author: Diana Kim
reviews.text: The entrance fee is free. It is a quiet and amazing place to visit. They also have a souvenir shop with affordable prices.

reviews.author: John Winter
reviews.text: We visited Long Son Pagoda on a weekend and were surprised to find it relatively quiet, which made the experience peaceful and enjoyable. The complex is beautifully maintained, with ornate temple architecture, bonsai-lined walkways, and a welcoming atmosphere. Be prepared for a bit of a climb — there are quite a few steps to reach the giant seated Buddha statue at the top, but it’s well worth the effort. The views over Nha Trang from up there are incredible, offering a panoramic perspective of the city skyline, mountains, and coast. About halfway up, you’ll find a serene reclining Buddha statue, carved into the hillside, surrounded by carved monk figures. It’s a calm spot to take a break before continuing making your way down. There is also a bell tower as well to see.

reviews.author: reuz
reviews.text: A temple located in the middle of Nha Trang city that cost 30k / person. The main attraction is the sleeping Buddha (low effort high reward because you just need to walk up one long flight of stairs) and the white Buddha (which you can see from the entrance but you have to walk 1-3 long fought of stairs). Nothing much to do up there but a good place to snap some pictures. We had fresh coconuts at the entrance. There are some shady uncles waiting to get tourists on the motorbikes to bring them up and down for 50k. Price is kinda steep and I think the steps are somewhat manageable. It's not a very big temple but I would pay it next time cause my upper thighs BURN and HURT for days to come

reviews.author: Y.H. Kang
reviews.text: 190 steps to the big buddha. Be sure to wear sneakers. Steps are steep at times. no flip flops or sandals

reviews.author: Tee
reviews.text: The giant Buddha was breathtaking. There’s also a reclining Buddha with carvings that were so intricate in detail. It was beautiful. There’s also a golden Buddha and a lovely garden. There was no cost to enter. Being that it is a temple, it is advised to dress respectfully.

reviews.author: Sandra Pfeiffer
reviews.text: Ok, it's a big Buddha. Other than that it's become just another market place to sell more stuff to people. I can imagine this isn't what the builders had in mind.... A little sad.

reviews.author: Alex Pipe
reviews.text: Very peaceful and beautiful place. The big white Buddha at the top is definitely worth the climb — the view is really impressive! The stairs aren’t too difficult, but take it slow if it’s hot. On the way up, there’s a reclining Buddha and some nice statues to check out. Entrance is free, but you might meet people trying to sell incense or ask for donations — just ignore them if you’re not interested, no pressure. Tip: Dress respectfully (no short shorts or tank tops), and try to visit early in the morning or late afternoon to avoid the heat and crowds. Nice spot for pictures and a little break from the usual touristy stuff.

reviews.author: Danelle Strydom
reviews.text: Very beautiful, but the people selling incense was really persistent and would not take no for an answer, and they get mad if you do not buy from them. When going to any pagoda, temple or holy place, wear clothes that cover your knees and shoulders out of respect.

reviews.author: Nathan Kitchen
reviews.text: A sobering and breathtaking memorial. Beautiful views of the city. Easy to get to, but there are LOTS of stairs.

reviews.author: Azima Isagalieva
reviews.text: Free entrance. Even it was mentioned no shorts and sleevelesses, everyone was dressed so, but i assume this only applies to the inside of the building, not the temple itself. There's some construction going on at the entrance. There are a lot of tourists inside, and of course, it's stuffy. But the statue is truly enormous. It's worth it.

reviews.author: Sunny
reviews.text: We went there in the evening, when the temperature was a little lower. Some people advised not to buy incense sticks when you just enter the temple, because they ask too much money, but later, when we climbed up, there was no place to buy them. The Buddha was beautiful and definitely worth visiting. Bring a mosquito repellent with you!!!

reviews.author: Bram Z
reviews.text: Easy to reach and definitely worth a visit when in the area, although I would not go out of my way specifically for it (as a tourist staying 3 days). Nonetheless happy to have been and observed it. It does get very hot up the hill on a sunny day and prepare for some stairs to climb when wanting to see the statue.

reviews.author: Youna Lee
reviews.text: There are many stairs so u can work out. U can have sugar cane juice on the top of the stairs and it's not that expensive. Statue is huge and express serenity vibe.

reviews.author: Ildus Batkayev
reviews.text: A peaceful and impressive spiritual site. The giant white Buddha gazing over the city is truly awe-inspiring, and the reclining Buddha is enormous — a breathtaking sight to see. The atmosphere is calm and respectful, perfect for a quiet walk and reflection. Plenty of photo-worthy moments along the way, with great views of Nha Trang from the top. A must-visit for those seeking both culture and serenity.
